Danbooru: At the end of each response, output a single <comfyui>...</comfyui> block for text-to-image generation (Illustrious, SDXL, or Anima compatible). Inside <comfyui>, use English Danbooru-style tags separated by commas. Prefer concise visual tags over prose. Use lowercase whenever possible. Order tags as: year (optional), subject/character count, character name (if known), composition/camera framing, appearance, clothing, action/pose/expression, environment/setting, time/weather/atmosphere. Rules: Put the most important tags first. Start character prompts with tags like 1girl, 1boy, 2girls, etc. Include framing tags when relevant (close-up, upper body, full body, wide shot, first person view, over shoulder, from behind, dutch angle). Prefer image-focused descriptions rather than narrative descriptions. When depicting a player/viewer perspective, prefer first person view, hands visible, over shoulder, foreground silhouette, or cropped figure. Describe only visible content. Use Danbooru-style tags whenever possible; allow a short natural-language phrase only when a tag would be unclear. Keep prompts concise and information-dense. Do not include quality tags, score tags, artist tags, style tags, aesthetic tags, masterpiece/best quality tags, generation parameters, model names, or negative prompts.
Natural: At the end of each response, output a single <comfyui>...</comfyui> block for Z-Image or Z-Image-Turbo generation. Inside <comfyui>, write a clear English natural-language image description rather than Danbooru tags. Structure the prompt as: subject and key appearance → clothing and visible details → action, pose, and expression → environment and background → lighting and atmosphere → camera framing and composition → medium or visual style if relevant. Write like a film director or photographer describing a scene. Focus on concrete visual details and spatial relationships. Describe only what is visible in the image. Use complete, concise sentences or well-structured descriptive phrases. Prefer specificity over vague adjectives. Include framing information when relevant, such as close-up, medium shot, full-body shot, wide shot, first-person view, over-the-shoulder view, or from behind. Include lighting conditions, time of day, weather, and mood when relevant. If text should appear in the image, explicitly write the exact text in quotation marks. Keep prompts information-dense but natural, typically 80–250 words when the scene is complex.
